refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle → operator feedback) over sustained operational periods. Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit under agreed conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator control commands to the remote vehicle with minimal loss.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle telemetry and sensor data back to the operator with minimal loss.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss, latency) to the operator and safety systems.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement network resilience mechanisms (redundancy, error correction, adaptive bitrate) to maintain packet loss within acceptable limits.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement end-to-end packet loss monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) control with redundant network paths and forward error correction (FEC) to maintain packet loss ≤1%.
DP-B: Deploy dual-path communication architecture (primary + backup network link) with automatic failover and packet loss detection to meet the ≤1% criterion.
DP-C: Implement application-level retransmission and acknowledgment protocol with adaptive timeout and redundant encoding to achieve ≤1% packet loss.
DP-D: Utilize customer-provided network infrastructure with passive monitoring of packet loss; escalate to Safety Services if threshold exceeded.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement end-to-end packet loss monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) control with redundant network paths and forward error correction (FEC) to maintain packet loss ≤1%.

potential_sources: DP-A couples teleoperation control logic (Tasking interface) with network infrastructure (Network Link interface) and safety monitoring (SafetyAlert interface). This is acceptable because packet loss is inherently a cross-layer concern requiring coordination between application, transport, and physical layers.
DP-A does not create unintended coupling with vehicle dynamics, payload management, or other non-teleoperation FRs.
coupling_check.notes: The coupling is intentional and necessary. Packet loss mitigation requires visibility across the entire communication stack and coordination with safety systems. No decoupling is recommended.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined network conditions are clearly specified and stable during acceptance testing.
ASSUMPTION: Redundant network paths (primary + backup) are available or can be provisioned by the customer.
ASSUMPTION: FEC overhead (increased bandwidth/latency) is acceptable within the teleoperation operational envelope.
RISK: If network conditions are highly variable or unpredictable, adaptive QoS may not reliably maintain ≤1% packet loss.
RISK: FEC adds latency; if operator control loop is time-critical, increased latency may degrade usability or safety.
RISK: Redundant paths increase system complexity and cost; customer may not accept this trade-off.
RISK: High safety criticality means packet loss failures could lead to loss of control; mitigation strategy must be robust and verifiable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with customer: (1) acceptable latency budget for teleoperation control loop, (2) availability of redundant network paths, (3) tolerance for FEC overhead. If latency is critical, consider hybrid approach: FEC for telemetry (one-way, latency-tolerant) and retransmission for control commands (bidirectional, latency-sensitive). Document network conditions and acceptance test plan as part of DP specification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end packet loss monitoring, adaptive QoS control, redundant network path management, and FEC implementation. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking for control commands and Telemetry for sensor feedback), Safety Services (via SafetyAlert for communication quality degradation), and the network infrastructure (via Network Link).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends operator control commands to Vehicle. Packet loss on this interface directly impacts operator control authority. QoS monitoring and FEC applied to this outbound path.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle telemetry and sensor data from Vehicle. Packet loss on this interface impacts operator situational awareness. QoS monitoring and FEC applied to this inbound path.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports communication quality degradation (packet loss threshold exceeded, latency anomalies) to Safety Services. Enables safety system to trigger operator alerts or autonomous safeguards.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module manages redundant network paths, monitors packet loss, applies FEC, and adapts QoS parameters. Bidirectional interface for both command transmission and telemetry reception.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation for teleoperation communication. All QoS, FEC, and redundancy logic is contained within this module. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces are to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link. This is the correct architectural choice.

Vehicle module owns vehicle dynamics and control, not communication quality. Assigning packet loss mitigation to Vehicle would create high diffusion (communication logic scattered across vehicle control logic) and violate separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and response, not communication infrastructure. While Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert notifications about packet loss, it should not own the QoS/FEC implementation. Assigning DP-A to Safety Services would create diffusion and coupling. Not recommended.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning packet loss mitigation here would bypass the Teleoperation module and create diffusion across multiple sub-systems. Not recommended; Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the correct architectural owner for DP-A (packet loss monitoring, adaptive QoS, redundant paths, FEC). This module naturally encapsulates teleoperation-specific communication logic and interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and network infrastructure (via Network Link). Containment score of 95 reflects high cohesion and minimal diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: If redundant network paths are managed by external infrastructure (e.g., customer's network operations), Teleoperation module may lack full control over path selection and failover. Mitigation: define clear API/contract with Network Link interface for path management.

RISK: FEC implementation may require low-level access to packet structure; if Vehicle module encapsulates packet format, Teleoperation may not have visibility. Mitigation: ensure Telemetry and Tasking interfaces expose packet-level metadata needed for FEC.
RISK: Safety Services may need to override QoS parameters in emergency scenarios (e.g., force safe shutdown). Mitigation: define SafetyAlert interface to support bidirectional control signals.
RISK: High safety criticality means packet loss failures must be detectable and reportable; Teleoperation module must have robust logging and diagnostics. Ensure this is part of module design.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has sufficient authority over Network Link interface to implement redundant path management and FEC. Define clear ownership boundaries: Teleoperation owns QoS logic and FEC; network infrastructure (external to NGHE) owns physical path provisioning. Document SafetyAlert interface to ensure Safety Services can receive and act on communication quality degradation alerts. Add diagnostic/logging requirements to Teleoperation module specification to support safety case and troubleshooting.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (adaptive QoS, redundant paths, FEC) directly addresses the refined FR (maintain packet loss ≤1%). The DP is specific, measurable, and implementable. The coupling check confirms that the DP does not create unintended cross-cutting concerns. Assumptions and risks are documented and manageable with proper design and testing.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must interface with external Network Link infrastructure; ensure API is well-defined and stable.
FEC and QoS logic must not leak into Vehicle module; maintain clear separation via Tasking/Telemetry interfaces.
SafetyAlert interface must be bidirectional to support safety system overrides; ensure this is designed into Teleoperation module.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-A. All core logic (QoS monitoring, FEC, redundancy) is owned by Teleoperation. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. No diffusion detected. Architectural packaging is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Packet loss can be measured end-to-end via instrumentation of Tasking and Telemetry interfaces. Acceptance test can simulate network conditions (latency, jitter, packet loss) and verify that Teleoperation module maintains ≤1% loss under agreed conditions. Feasible with standard network testing tools.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-009 is allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (009.1-009.4) are allocated to Teleoperation sub-components (command transmission, telemetry reception, quality monitoring, resilience mechanisms). Clear allocation hierarchy supports verification and traceability.

recommended_revisions: Document network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, topology) as part of acceptance criteria. Ensure customer and system engineer agree on these conditions before design finalization.
Define SafetyAlert interface contract: what packet loss thresholds trigger alerts, what data is included in alerts, how Safety Services responds.
Specify FEC algorithm and overhead budget (bandwidth, latency, CPU). Ensure overhead is acceptable within teleoperation operational envelope.
Add diagnostic/logging requirements to Teleoperation module: packet loss metrics, QoS adaptation history, failover events. Support safety case and troubleshooting.
Plan for acceptance testing: network simulation environment, measurement methodology, acceptance criteria verification procedure.

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-009 is well-defined and non-atomic. It has been decomposed into four sub-FRs (009.1-009.4) that address distinct aspects of packet loss mitigation: command transmission, telemetry reception, quality monitoring, and resilience mechanisms. Each sub-FR requires further design and implementation detail. The selected DP-A is sound and properly allocated to the Teleoperation module. The next level of decomposition should detail how each sub-FR is realized within Teleoperation (e.g., FEC algorithm selection, redundant path failover logic, QoS adaptation strategy). Proceed with functional decomposition of sub-FRs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator control commands to the remote vehicle with minimal loss.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle telemetry and sensor data back to the operator with minimal loss.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss, latency) to the operator and safety systems.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement network resilience mechanisms (redundancy, error correction, adaptive bitrate) to maintain packet loss within acceptable limits.

assumptions: Customer-defined network conditions are clearly specified and stable during acceptance testing.
Redundant network paths (primary + backup) are available or can be provisioned by the customer.
FEC overhead (increased bandwidth/latency) is acceptable within the teleoperation operational envelope.
Teleoperation module has sufficient authority over Network Link interface to implement redundant path management.
Safety Services can receive and act on SafetyAlert notifications about communication quality degradation.
Operator control loop latency budget accommodates FEC and adaptive QoS mechanisms.
open_risks: If network conditions are highly variable or unpredictable, adaptive QoS may not reliably maintain ≤1% packet loss.
FEC adds latency; if operator control loop is time-critical, increased latency may degrade usability or safety.
Redundant paths increase system complexity and cost; customer may not accept this trade-off.
High safety criticality means packet loss failures could lead to loss of control; mitigation strategy must be robust and verifiable.
If Vehicle module encapsulates packet format, Teleoperation may lack visibility for FEC implementation.
evidence_needed: Customer specification of network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, topology) and acceptance criteria.
Network simulation environment and test plan for acceptance testing.
FEC algorithm selection and overhead analysis (bandwidth, latency, CPU impact).
SafetyAlert interface contract and Safety Services response procedures.
Teleoperation module design specification detailing QoS monitoring, FEC, redundancy, and failover logic.
Verification plan for packet loss measurement and acceptance criteria validation.
Safety case documentation addressing packet loss failure modes and mitigation strategies.
